title: Spider-silk-like shape memory polymer fiber for vibration damping
abstract: In this study, the static and dynamic properties of shape memory polyurethane (SMPU) fiber are reported and compared to those of spider dragline silk. Although the polymeric fiber has a lower strength compared to spider dragline silks (0.2–0.3 GPa versus 1.1 GPa), it possesses much higher toughness (276–289 MJ m<sup>-3</sup> versus 160 MJ m<sup>-3</sup>), due to its excellent extensibility. The dynamic mechanical tests reveal that SMPU fiber has a high damping capacity (tan  $b4$ =0.10–0.35) which is comparable to or even higher than that of spider silks (tan  $b4$ =0.15). In addition, we found that, different programming methods change the shape memory and damping properties of the fiber in different ways and cold-drawing programming is more advocated in structural applications. These results suggest that the SMPU fiber has similar vibration damping and mechanical properties as spider silk, and may find applications in lightweight engineering structures.
results:
3.1. DSC Analysis and programming effects

3.1.1. General description of morphology based on DSC results. The morphologies of polyurethane copolymers have been studied for decades and many morphological models have been proposed based on these results [29, 34-39]. Generally speaking, the polymer is composed of pure hard phase, pure soft phase, and a mixed phase, in which hard and soft segments incorporated with each other. Figure 2(a) is a schematic representation of the morphological model as a visual aid for the reader. Depending on the hard and soft segment contents, the molecular weight and distribution, and the thermomechanical history, the general three-phase model may take different configurations. For example, the pure soft phase may disappear for some SMPUs, the pure hard phase may become the continuous phase, the three-phase may become a random mixture, etc. It is the versatile microstructures that make SMPUs have such diverse physical/mechanical properties.

3.1.2. Programming effects. In order to clarify the complete procedure of the programming-recovery cycle, stress–strain time 3D plots for both samples #2 and #3 are shown in figures 3(a) and (b), respectively (see the 2D plots in supplementary materials figure S3 and S4 for more details). In the programming processes, for sample #2, it was first stretched to 100% at a rate of 2 mm min<sup>-1</sup> and held for one hour while maintaining the 100% strain at 100 °C (steps 1-2 in figure 3(a)). It was then cooled down to room temperature while holding the strain constant before releasing from the fixtures and relaxing in stress free condition (steps 3-5 in figure 3(a)). For sample #3, it was also first stretched to 100% at a rate of 2 mm min<sup>-1</sup> and held for one hour while maintaining the strain at room temperature (steps 1-2 in figure 3(b)). It was then unloaded from the fixtures and relaxed in stress free condition until the shape was stabilized (steps 3-4 in figure 3(b)). This completed programming of both samples. During this procedure, molecular chains were forced to rotate, extend and even slide against each other, and rearranged into a new microstructural configuration. This microstructural evolution was reflected in the macro-scale as stress relaxation (steps 2-3 in figure 3(a) and step 2 in figure 3), which resulted in the formation of 'temporary shape' and storage of stress. During the fully constrained recovery processes (step 6 in figure 3(a) and step 5 in figure 3(b)), the tendency for the molecular chains to restore the original configuration leads to releasing of 'stored stress' [41, 42].
discussion:
3.4. Comparison with spider silk

3.4.1. Mechanical properties. The mechanical properties of dragline silk of several different species have been extensively investigated [61–64]. Most spider silks have an outstanding combination of strength and extensibility, which yields a very high toughness (the amount of energy absorbed per volume before breakage) that exceeds most natural or man-made fibers. A comparison in mechanical properties, including strength, extensibility and toughness of the SMPU fiber with spider dragline silk and other common structural materials are given in table 1 below [10, 21, 65]. The strength of the SMPU fiber is close to, but still lower than that of the spider silk. However, due to its excellent extensibility, SMPU fiber has the highest toughness among these materials. In other words, it can absorb the highest amount of energy before fracture.

3.4.2. Super-contraction forces. The shape recoverability of shape memory polymer has been extensively studied for years and great achievement has been attained on this aspect [14, 16, 67, 68]. However, in some applications, such as self-healing and actuator, stress recovery is of great importance. Most recent studies aimed at increasing the recovery forces of shape memory polymer. The strategy includes: post-spinning methods [31, 69]; adding carbon nanotubes [70, 71]; tailoring the hard segment properties by synthesizing hybrid diol [72]; etc.

3.4.3. Damping properties. Table 2 [1, 79] provides a damping property comparison of SMPU fiber with spider dragline silk, and some other representative materials. According to the data provided, SMPU fiber has a very high damping capability comparable to or even higher than that of spider silks and at the same time possesses a fairly high storage modulus. Besides, as demonstrated above, the simple programming procedures not only change the glass transition temperature and the mechanical properties, provide the material with shape memory function, but also improve the damping capability. All these features will give this material a promising future.
conclusion:
Based on the above tests, measurement and characterization, the following conclusions can be drawn:

- (1) Similar to spider silk, our SMPU fiber possesses high damping ability, and at the same time has a fairly high strength and toughness. In addition, the polymer fiber has shape memory effect, and after tension programming, has super-contraction upon heating, similar to moisture triggered spider silk. These similarities between spider silk and SMPU fiber are determined by their resemblances in microstructure.
- (2) The shape memory property is characterized by the fully constrained recovery stress. Different programming procedures result in different recovery behaviors. The cold-drawn fiber (sample #3) can generate higher stabilized stress, with is desirable in self-healing applications.
- (3) Programming the polymer fiber can alter its mechanical and thermal properties. After programming, stiffness and strength can be increased while extensibility is decreased. Programming at high temperature (sample #2) lowers glass transition temperature and the overall damping ability. On the contrary, programming at low temperature (sample #3) increases the glass transition temperature and damping ability. These differences attribute to the energy dissipated by the recovery stress triggered during damping tests.
